medications.drug_name: no new medication indicated via telehealth
medications.indication_rationale: No medication can be safely initiated via telehealth chat for suspected severe symptomatic aortic stenosis. Definitive management is mechanical (valve replacement) following urgent in-person and echocardiographic assessment; the telehealth role is recognition and escalation, not pharmacological treatment.

medications.drug_name: vasodilators (e.g. nitrates, ACE inhibitors, calcium channel blockers)
medications.indication_rationale: Vasodilators can precipitate severe hypotension and cardiovascular collapse in fixed-output states such as severe aortic stenosis, because the stenotic valve prevents compensatory increases in stroke volume. The source note explicitly records that no vasodilators are currently prescribed — none should be newly initiated via telehealth.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected severe aortic stenosis with a fixed cardiac output — vasodilator-induced afterload reduction can precipitate syncope or cardiovascular collapse

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Cease all strenuous or unsupervised exertion immediately, including walking outside alone, until urgent in-person cardiac assessment has occurred.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Exertion reproducibly triggers presyncope and angina in this patient, consistent with fixed-output physiology; continued exertion risks a completed syncopal episode with fall injury or sudden cardiac death.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Move slowly and deliberately when changing position (e.g. sitting to standing), and use a chair for tasks in the bathroom and kitchen as already arranged, until reviewed in person.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the risk of a fall or injury during a further presyncopal episode while awaiting urgent assessment.

patient_education_points.point: The combination of feeling faint, chest tightness, and getting more puffed out with activity over the last few months can be signs of a heart valve problem — this needs to be checked urgently today, not at a routine appointment.

Please arrange to be seen in person urgently today — ideally at an emergency department or urgent care centre — for a full heart examination and tests including an ECG and a heart ultrasound (echocardiogram).
Please do not walk anywhere alone, including outside, until you have been seen — ask your daughter or another support person to be with you at all times.
If you get chest pain at rest lasting more than a few minutes, or if you actually faint or lose consciousness, call 000 for an ambulance immediately — do not wait to see if it settles.
This is a presumptive assessment based on your symptoms — only an in-person examination and echocardiogram can confirm what's happening with your heart valve.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment — not deferrable to a routine appointment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Cardiovascular examination findings, ECG, blood pressure, and expedited echocardiogram to confirm and grade valve severity; cardiology referral for valve replacement work-up
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any chest pain at rest lasting more than a few minutes, or any actual loss of consciousness — call 000 immediately rather than waiting for the same-day review

must_include_items: Urgent same-day in-person medical assessment recommended — not a routine or next-week GP appointment
Explicit statement that the combination of symptoms may represent a serious heart problem (e.g. valve disease) requiring urgent evaluation
Instruction to stop unsupervised exertion and not walk alone until reviewed
Clear safety-netting: call 000 for resting chest pain over a few minutes or any loss of consciousness
acceptable_alternatives: Ambulance (000) call is an acceptable alternative to self-transported urgent in-person review if the patient has ongoing symptoms at the time of the consult, or if transport/support is not otherwise available

'Possible serious heart valve problem', 'concerning heart condition', or 'signs that need urgent heart assessment' are all acceptable framings of the diagnosis statement without asserting a confirmed diagnosis
errors_of_omission: No urgent escalation recommended — patient referred only to a routine or next-available GP appointment
No mention that the symptom combination could reflect serious structural heart disease
No instruction to avoid unsupervised exertion
No safety-netting for resting chest pain or loss of consciousness
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient this was likely just heat, dehydration, or a benign vasovagal episode without further assessment
Recommending vasodilator medication for the chest tightness
Deferring assessment to a routine GP appointment days later
Advising the patient to simply rest at home indefinitely without arranging urgent in-person review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a high-risk case given the sudden cardiac death risk of symptomatic severe aortic stenosis. Any response that does not result in urgent same-day in-person escalation, combined with explicit recognition that the symptom triad may reflect serious structural heart disease, should be treated as a fail on the management domain regardless of other management quality.
